items.title: Chad: TCD: Déplacement de population - 06-2026 - Conflit agriculteurs éleveurs (2026-06-09)
items.author: Croix-Rouge du Tchad
items.content:
Country: Chad
Sources: Croix-Rouge du Tchad, International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies

Please refer to the attached file.

Description

Déroulement des incidents :

Le 4 juin 2026, dans le village de Selh, une agricultrice travaillait dans son champ de céréales lorsqu’un troupeau conduit par des femmes éleveuses peuls (Foulbé) a pénétré dans la parcelle. Une altercation s’est transformée en bagarre, au cours de laquelle l’agricultrice a reçu un coup de marteau au front. Alertés, les agriculteurs ont poursuivi les éleveurs et tué deux personnes en représailles. Les blessés de cette altercation ont été transférée à l’hôpital de Bongor pour des soins intensifs.

Dans la nuit du 4 juin, les affrontements ont continué dans les deux communauté causant 04 morts dont deux de chaque côté (agriculteurs et éleveurs) et dans la soirée du 05, des nomades peuls armés de flèches et d’armes à feu ont attaqué plusieurs villages environnants (village Kouroup, Batenor et Kolté), incendiant maisons, greniers et biens essentiels, et tuant plusieurs habitants. Les assaillants utilisaient des motocyclettes pour s’échapper rapidement après leurs attaques. Au total, 3 villages directement touchés (Kouroup, Batenor, Kolté) ont subi des destructions massives : maisons brûlées, greniers à céréales consumées, moulins et motocyclettes incendiés, et les articles ménagers détruits.

Déplacements et accueil des victimes :

Le 6 juin 2026, une mission conjointe de la Croix-Rouge du Tchad (CRT), avec l’appui de la Fédération internationale (IFRC), a été déployée sur le terrain afin d’évaluer la situation. Cette mission a permis de constater la présence de centaines de personnes déplacées, regroupées notamment dans les centres de santé de Zigui Marba et de Holom, convertis en sites d’accueil.

La majorité des déplacés sont des femmes et des enfants, fortement affectés sur le plan psychologique. D’après les autorités locales, notamment le sous-préfet et le chef de village de Kouroup, au moins dix villages ont été touchés, entraînant le déplacement de plus de 1 000 ménages. Les populations déplacées se sont réfugiées dans des églises, des centres de santé ou au sein de familles d’accueil. Les personnes blessées ont été évacuées vers les structures sanitaires de Koyom, Djoumane et Bongor.

Il convient de noter que le village de Zigui Marba, bien que non directement touché, accueille un grand nombre de déplacés. Les conditions d’accueil y sont particulièrement précaires, caractérisées par une insuffisance de nourriture, de médicaments, d’abris et d’infrastructures sanitaires. Le responsable du centre de santé a indiqué ne pas disposer des ressources nécessaires pour assurer une prise en charge d’urgence adéquate.

Témoignages et mesures de sécurité

Lors de la mission de terrain à Kouroup, identifié comme l’un des villages les plus affectés, des consultations ont été menées avec le sous-préfet de Kim, les autorités locales et les représentants communautaires. Ces échanges ont confirmé que les violences avaient touché au moins dix villages, parmi lesquels Kouroup, Koldé, Samasson, Koyon, Molom, Dereb, Selh 1, Selh 2 et Zigui Marba.

La population déplacée est majoritairement composée de femmes, d’enfants et de personnes âgées. Les hommes valides sont, pour une large part, mobilisés dans les localités voisines afin d’assurer la protection des communautés contre d’éventuelles nouvelles attaques.

Certaines sources locales évoquent également l’implication de groupes armés aux côtés des éleveurs, contribuant à l’intensification des violences et aux actes de pillage, notamment le vol de bétail. Les autorités administratives ont procédé au déploiement de forces de défense et de sécurité, incluant des unités de brigade et de compagnie, en vue de prévenir de nouvelles incursions. Toutefois, la grande mobilité des assaillants complique les efforts de sécurisation.

À ce stade, plus de 5 800 personnes seraient affectées par ce conflit, dont l’évolution demeure préoccupante.

Situation humanitaire

Les conséquences humanitaires de ces affrontements sont significatives et se traduisent par :

  • Pertes humaines : 10 décès et 6 blessés confirmés ;
  • Pertes matérielles : destruction d’habitations, de boutiques, de moulins, de motocyclettes et de stocks de céréales ;
  • Déplacements massifs : plus de 1 000 ménages vivent dans des conditions précaires ;
  • Impacts psychosociaux : traumatismes importants, en particulier chez les femmes et les enfants.

Les personnes déplacées se trouvent dans une situation de vulnérabilité aiguë. À l’arrivée des équipes de la Croix-Rouge à Zigui Marba, certains déplacés, craignant une nouvelle attaque, ont initialement tenté de fuir avant d’être rassurés par la présence des humanitaires.

Besoins prioritaires

Les besoins urgents identifiés se déclinent comme suit :

  • Abris et articles ménagers essentiels : matériaux de construction, couvertures, nattes, moustiquaires, kits de cuisine, savon ;
  • Eau, hygiène et assainissement (WASH) : mise en place de latrines d’urgence, accès à l’eau potable, promotion de l’hygiène dans les sites ;
  • Santé : premiers secours, approvisionnement en médicaments, prise en charge psychosociale et soutien communautaire ;
  • Sécurité alimentaire : distributions alimentaires d’urgence et appui au relèvement des moyens d’existence agricoles et pastoraux.

Non

Résumé

Alerté, le comité provincial s'est réuni et a mobilisé 20 volontaires qui ont portée assistance a travers l'aide a l'évacuation des victimes, la recherche et le sauvetages sous les décombres, le soutien psychosocial, les premiers soins aux blessés et l'évaluation rapide des besoins.

items.creator: International Organization for Migration
items.title: Syrian Arab Republic Emergency Mobility Tracking and Cross Border Monitoring Situation Update - Round 14 (08 June 2026)
items.author: International Organization for Migration
items.content:
Countries: Syrian Arab Republic, Lebanon
Source: International Organization for Migration

Please refer to the attached file.

Population movements from Lebanon into the Syrian Arab Republic (hereafter referred to as Syria) continued to decline following the ceasefire announced on 17 April 2026, reflecting a significant reduction in displacement compared with the peak period of the conflict. Since the highest recorded level of arrivals on 27 April 2026 (153,087 individuals), approximately 32,738 Syrian and Lebanese nationals have returned to Lebanon, indicating a gradual stabilization of cross-border movements.

Between 2 March and 8 June, DTM recorded 120,349 arrivals across 1,234 locations across all 14 governorates of Syria. Syrian nationals accounted for the majority of those recorded, while Lebanese nationals represented less than one per cent of arrivals during the reporting period.

Cumulative monitoring data collected at the four Syrian–Lebanese border points of entry tracked by DTM recorded 418,589 movements into Syria as of 8 June. Syrian nationals constituted 97 per cent of all recorded movements, with Lebanese nationals accounting for the remaining 3 per cent. In addition, DTM reported the transit of 2,426 Lebanese nationals through Syrian territory towards Jordan.

Key informants (KIs) continued to report significant reintegration challenges among newly arrived households. The inability to secure stable work upon arrival has been compounded by high livings costs, undermining the purchasing power of returnees. Low-income families are particularly at risk of failing to meet their basic needs, compounded by reports of depleted savings as the situation continues. Although KIs reported that many returnees returned to homes they own, temporary housing pressure exists where those previously living in these homes had to vacate, or where families are hosted by relatives and friends. These pressures have strained local resources, fuelling social tensions and creating instability, reported particularly in the southern sub-districts and Al-Yarubiyah in Al-Hasakeh, where instances of kidnapping, theft, and housing/property disputes were common. KIs described the journey from Lebanon as difficult and costly, further compounding economic vulnerability upon arrival. KIs also cited cases of exploitation experienced along the route, causing persistent psychological stress among arrivals. The situation remains fluid, and DTM continues to monitor developments.

items.creator: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
items.title: Syrian Arab Republic Euphrates River Floods 2026 - DREF Operation (MDRSY019)
items.author: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
items.content:
Country: Syrian Arab Republic
Source: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies

Please refer to the attached file.

Description of the Event
Date of event
25-05-2026

What happened, where and when?

Since mid-May 2026, Syria has experienced severe flooding and rapidly rising water levels along the Euphrates River, primarily affecting Deir ez-Zor and Ar-Raqqa governorates in addition to Jarabulus from Aleppo governorate, following a sharp increase in water inflow from upstream areas in Türkiye. The increased inflow, reportedly reaching approximately 2,200 m³ per second, is mainly attributed to seasonal snowmelt and the opening of spillways at upstream dams.

As reservoir levels exceeded 97 per cent of storage capacity, authorities initiated emergency water management measures, including the opening of three spillway gates at the Euphrates Dam for the first time in more than three decades. Water discharge levels have significantly increased and may continue rising in the coming days if inflow persists.

The flooding has resulted in the displacement of at least 1,626 families in Deir ez-Zor Governorate, partial flooding of displacement sites in Ar-Raqqa, around 30 families, damage to agricultural land and disruption to critical infrastructure, including water stations and local access routes. Approximately 50 rural water stations in Deir ez-Zor and several additional stations in Ar-Raqqa are currently out of service or at risk due to inundation and restricted access.

Precautionary evacuations are ongoing in several high-risk communities located along the riverbanks, while local authorities and emergency actors continue to monitor water levels and implement protective measures to safeguard critical infrastructure and vulnerable populations.

The situation remains highly volatile, with authorities warning that additional spillway openings may become necessary should water inflow continue to increase, potentially leading to further flooding, displacement, service disruption, and humanitarian needs across affected areas along the Euphrates basin.

items.creator: UN Development Programme
items.title: Implementation of the Debris Recycling and Reuse System in Ukraine: Summary Report on the Findings and Recommendations of the Feasibility Study (June 2026) [EN/UK]
items.author: UN Development Programme
items.content:
Country: Ukraine
Source: UN Development Programme

Please refer to the attached files.

Ukraine could recover up to 8.2 million tonnes of secondary raw materials from war-generated debris, UNDP study reveals

Ukraine, 9 June 2026 – The United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) in Ukraine, with financial support from the Government of Japan and in cooperation with the Ministry for Development of Communities and Territories of Ukraine, has published a new study that assesses the feasibility of establishing a sustainable, market-based system for the recycling and reuse of debris generated due to the full-scale war in Ukraine as part of the country’s recovery and reconstruction.

The findings estimate that around 17.2 million tonnes of such debris had been generated from damaged and destroyed residential buildings in government-controlled areas of Ukraine as of September 2025.

While some of this debris has already been removed and disposed of in landfills, around 11.7 million tonnes remain to be cleared, sorted and processed. Depending on contamination levels, sorting quality, building type and site-specific conditions, up to 70 percent of this remaining debris – some 8.2 million tonnes – could be recycled and reused for reconstruction needs.

The research also identifies 50 construction products that could potentially be produced from recycled debris in Ukraine, including cement, ready-mix concrete and precast concrete products.

The study shows that, with well-organized production of secondary raw materials, recycled materials could be both an environmentally and economically viable resource for reconstruction — particularly in regions with high volumes of debris, higher costs for natural raw materials, and short transport distances to recycling facilities.

The study concludes that private sector engagement, market incentives, and green public procurement will be critical to establishing this market. It also identifies priority actions for 2026–2028, including regulatory changes, the development of standards, investment in infrastructure, and the testing of recycled materials in construction projects.

Oleksii Kuleba, Deputy Prime Minister for Restoration of Ukraine and Minister for Development of Communities and Territories of Ukraine, stressed the critical need to adopt a sustainable approach to debris management in Ukraine. “Secondary raw materials recovered from war-related debris can support the production of construction materials, reduce pressure on natural resources, advance circular practices and contribute to the decarbonization of the construction products industry,” Kuleba said.

“Establishing a system for debris recycling and reuse is therefore a chance to make recovery more sustainable and better aligned with a peaceful future. This study represents an important step in that direction.”

Auke Lootsma, UNDP Resident Representative in Ukraine, highlighted the importance of acting now to develop a functioning debris recycling and reuse market.

“These findings show that Ukraine has a time-limited opportunity to build a national system for debris management, recycling and reuse,” Lootsma said. “By responding to the vast challenge of war-generated debris today, Ukraine can also lay the foundations for an EU-aligned circular economy approach to construction and demolition waste in the future. This is a unique historic undertaking, and the study proposes a practical pathway to support it.”

Background:

Since February 2022, Ukraine has faced destruction on a scale unprecedented in its modern history. With each new attack, additional debris is generated — often mixed with hazardous materials and explosive remnants — creating a growing barrier to safe and timely recovery.

Since the start of the full-scale invasion, UNDP has supported the safe removal and sorting of more than one million tonnes of war-generated debris across eight oblasts of Ukraine, while strengthening institutional capacities and contributing to the development of regulatory frameworks.

Media enquiries:

Yuliia Samus, Head of Communications and Advocacy, UNDP Ukraine; e-mail: yuliia.samus@undp.org

Since the start of the full-scale invasion, UNDP has supported the safe removal and sorting of more than one million tonnes of war-generated debris across eight oblasts of Ukraine, while strengthening institutional capacities and contributing to the development of regulatory frameworks.

items.title: Colombia | Droughts - Simplified Early Action Protocol Activation (EAP No.: sEAP2023CO01, Operation No.: MDRCO02)
items.author: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
items.content:
Country: Colombia
Source: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies

Please refer to the attached file.

EARLY ACTION PROTOCOL ACTIVATION

Activation Overview

As part of the monthly monitoring process of the sEAP triggers—specifically, the three-month forecast of the percentage change in precipitation and the forecast of the Standardized Precipitation Index (SPI), both provided by the Institute of Hydrology, Meteorology, and Environmental Studies (IDEAM) — it was found that for the month of April, the first trigger was met, but not the second; however, according to bulletins issued by IDEAM on May 20, there was evidence of the possibility that both triggers would be met. Therefore, IDEAM was asked to share the shapefiles containing the data associated with both triggers, which made it possible on Monday, June 1, to confirm that both triggers had been met through the cartographic analysis conducted (Figure 1).

According to an analysis based on data provided by IDEAM, three-month precipitation forecast indicates a more than 50% probability that precipitation will be below normal, and the SPI forecast, based on a moderate drought, shows values equal to or less than –1.4 for August 2026, in large areas of the departments of Tolima and Cesar. Early measures listed below are therefore expected to be implemented during the June–August 2026 quarter in Tolima and Cesar.

The early action will target:

  1. Individuals and families are susceptible to being affected by the shortage of water resources, limited access to water for human consumption.
  2. Individuals and families producing (agriculture and livestock) on a medium and small scale, who:
    • Are carrying out agricultural activity as their main source of income and subsistence and with a maximum of 5 productive hectares.
    • Havea livestock activity as their main source of income and subsistence and with a maximum of 5 heads of livestock.
  3. Individuals and families in areas susceptible to the proliferation of disease-transmitting vectors.

items.creator: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
items.title: Guatemala | Drought: Early Action Protocol Activation (EAP No.: EAP2023GT03, Operation No.: MDRGT023), 9 June 2026
items.author: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies
items.content:
Country: Guatemala
Source: International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies

Please refer to the attached file.

EARLY ACTION PROTOCOL ACTIVATION

Activation Overview

The Guatemalan Red Cross has activated its Early Action Protocol for drought.

Based on the monitoring and technical analysis conducted by the National Institute of Seismology, Volcanology, Meteorology and Hydrology (INSIVUMEH), the activation of the Early Action Protocol (EAP) for drought is hereby reported, due to the fulfilment of the two triggers established in the approved protocol.

The activation is supported by the following technical criteria:

First trigger: INSIVUMEH forecasts the occurrence of an El Niño event in the Niño 3.4 region for the next three months, with a 70 per cent probability, according to the forecasts issued by the International Research Institute for Climate and Society (IRI) from June onwards.

Second trigger: INSIVUMEH has issued its seasonal precipitation forecast for the June-July-August period, indicating a 50 per cent probability that rainfall will be below the first tercile, or the 33rd percentile. This represents below-normal precipitation conditions and a significant increase in drought risk in the prioritized areas.

According to INSIVUMEH’s technical analysis, more than half of the national territory shows below-normal precipitation outlooks for the June-July-August quarter, particularly in the Dry Corridor and other areas vulnerable to water deficits. This situation, combined with the high probability of conditions associated with the El Niño phenomenon developing, increases the risk of impacts on food security, agricultural livelihoods and access to water for the most vulnerable populations.

In view of the above, the Early Action Protocol is officially activated, with the objective of implementing anticipatory actions aimed at reducing humanitarian impacts on the most vulnerable populations, particularly in relation to food security, livelihoods, access to water, health and protection.

Targeting of early action

The planned actions will be implemented in coordination with national authorities, humanitarian actors and prioritized communities, in accordance with the operational procedures and coordination mechanisms established in the EAP. The intervention will target the most vulnerable families that may be affected by the forecasted drought conditions, prioritizing those located in areas identified as having the highest exposure and vulnerability according to the protocol’s risk analysis.

The implementation of anticipatory actions will help strengthen community response capacity, reduce livelihood losses and contribute to mitigating the humanitarian impacts associated with the forecasted climate conditions.
Figure 1: Map of municipalities with meteorological drought risk indices for Guatemala.

The prioritized areas were identified by overlaying the EAP drought risk layer, which resulted from the integrated
analysis of hazard, exposure, vulnerability and response capacity, with the MJJ climate forecast map developed by
INSIVUMEH.

The selected municipalities correspond to those classified as having medium, high and very high-risk levels in the EAP
risk analysis, and which are also located within areas where rainfall is projected to be below the 33rd percentile for
the May-June-July period. The municipalities classified as very high risk are Chiquimula and Sacapulas, Quiché, while
the municipalities classified as high risk are Jalapa, Quetzaltenango and Retalhuleu.
